Description: A unique ID that can be used to identify an asset.
A term specific to the CIS Controls that can be used to classify an asset. Can also be customized based on the enterprise.
The purchase date of the asset.
The purchase price of the asset.
A general description of the enterprise asset.
The manufacturer of the asset.
The model number of the asset that identifies the type or product line. Given by the manufacturer.
The serial number of the asset that uniquely identifies the asset. Can be the same or different from the Enterprise Asset ID. Given by the manufacturer.

Term: Name of the Enterprise Asset Owner
Description: The member of the workforce that the enterprise asset is assigned to and is responsible for.
The name that is assigned to an enterprise asset for the purposes of identification and management.
The business unit or department that the enterprise asset owner resides in.
The physical location of the enterprise asset.
A 12-character hexadecimal number to uniquely identify an enterprise asset. Assigned by the manufacturer on the network interface card (NIC).

A network address assigned to an enterprise asset for identification within a network, either internal or external. While intended to be unique, IP addresses are often shared due to NAT (Network Address Translation) or proxying, meaning a single IP may represent multiple devices. This can create challenges for asset tracking, security monitoring, and network management tasks.
The expiration date of the warranty.
Any information that may be prudent to include regarding licensing.

Term: Approval to Connect to Enterprise Network?
Description: Is the asset approved to connect to the network.

Term: IT Verification of Enterprise Asset Inventory
Description: The last date that the asset was verified by IT.
The date that the asset was disposed of.
The method or type of disposal.
Example: Hard drive shredded through data destruction service on-site.

Description: The title/name of the software.
The business justification or purpose that the software is used for.
Example: Used as the enterprise's enterprise resource planning tool

Description: A term specific to the CIS Controls that can be used to classify an asset. Can also be customized based on the enterprise.
The developer or publisher of the software.
The acquisition date of the software.
The installation date of the software.
The duration that the software has been used since installation.
The URL/Link where more information can be found on the software.
If purchased through an App Store, the reference/link of the App Store where more information can be found on the application.
The version (or versions, if multiple) of the software.
The method of deployment for the software.

The date when the software is no longer supported for updates, including security patches, and should be retired.  Note that some software developers may provide an extension for certain legacy products as a short-term solution during migration to a newer, supported software version/platform.
The date that the asset is no longer in use.
Any information that may be prudent to include regarding licensing.
The number of licenses procured for each software asset.
An short name/identifier that can be used to refer to the data.
A term specific to the CIS Controls that can be used to classify data. Can also be customized based on the enterprise.
The type of data that is being inventoried.
A brief description of the data.
The location that the data resides - can be physical or digital.
The format of the data - how the data is arranged, stored, and/or transmitted.
The person responsible for the data.
The categorization that a piece of data, or dataset, is assigned, based on sensitivity and regulatory requirements (e.g., Public, Confidential, Restricted, Sensitive).
The business justification or purpose that the data is used for.
The length of time that the data will be stored by the enterprise.
If disposed, how the data is disposed - can be physical or digital.
The date that the data was disposed of.
The service provider or product associated with the account.
The member of the workforce that the account is assigned to and is responsible for.
A term specific to the CIS Controls that can be used to classify an account.
A unique identifier that can be used to log into software.
The date that the account was created.
The date that the account was deleted.
The date that the account expired.

Term: Account Status (Enabled, Disabled, Deleted, Dormant, Locked)
Description: The status of the account currently. Examples may include enabled, disabled, deleted, dormant, and locked.
Indicates if the account has multi-factor authentication (MFA) enabled or not. Note that not all accounts will be able to support MFA.
The last date that the account was validated.
The business unit or department that the account owner resides in.
The name of the service provider.
The classification of the service provider.
The point of contact at the service provider that is responsible for assisting the enterprise. Can have multiple contacts that provide various types of support (technical, sales, etc.)
The point of contact at the enterprise that is responsible for the service provider.
The business unit or department that is using the service provider.
A short description of what the service provider does for the enterprise.
The start date of the service provider contract.
The expiration date of the service provider contract.
The last date that the contract was reviewed.
